evals.name: discount-strategy-oo-design
evals.prompt: I'm building a checkout system for an online store. We need to apply different discount strategies: percentage off (e.g. 15%), buy-one-get-one-free, and a seasonal flat-amount coupon (e.g. $10 off). Right now I have a Checkout class with a giant if/else chain that checks a discountType string and applies the right math inline, and it's getting messy every time we add a new discount type. How should I structure this in Java? Show me the design.
evals.expected_output: A design using the strategy pattern with an interface (e.g. DiscountStrategy) and separate implementations per discount type, injected into Checkout via the interface (dependency inversion). No if/else type branching. Composition over inheritance. Mention of open-closed principle (adding a discount = new class, no edit to Checkout). Concise, focused interfaces.
expectations: Defines a strategy interface (e.g. DiscountStrategy or similar) rather than type-string branching
Each discount type is a separate class implementing the interface, not inlined in Checkout
Checkout depends on the interface, not concrete discount classes (dependency inversion)
Adding a new discount type does not require editing Checkout (open-closed principle)
No if/else or switch on a discount type string in the core logic

evals.name: functional-aggregation
evals.prompt: I have a List<Transaction> where Transaction has getAmount() returning BigDecimal and getCategory() returning a String. I need to compute the total amount per category and return the categories sorted by total descending, but only including categories whose total exceeds 1000. Write this in Java.
evals.expected_output: A declarative stream pipeline: collect with groupingBy(getCategory, summingBigDecimal), filter entries over 1000, sort by value descending. Immutable, no mutation inside intermediate ops, meaningful parameter names, no unnecessary defensive code beyond the requirements.
expectations: Uses a stream pipeline (declarative), not imperative for-loops with mutable accumulators
Groups by category and sums amounts per group (groupingBy + reduction/sum)
Does not mutate external state inside filter/map intermediate operations
Uses meaningful parameter names, not single letters

evals.name: refactor-imperative-to-design
evals.expected_output: Identifies type-branching (violates OCP/SRP), low cohesion (one method doing dispatch + validation + sending + audit). Refactors to a Notifier interface with EmailNotifier/SmsNotifier/PushNotifier implementations, NotificationService depends on a collection of Notifiers looked up by type (DIP). Validation pushed into each notifier (LSP/substitutability). Audit as a cross-cutting step. Composed methods at one level of abstraction (SLAP).
expectations: Identifies the if/else type-branching as an open-closed principle violation
Introduces a Notifier (or similar) interface with one implementation per channel
NotificationService depends on the interface, not concrete channels (dependency inversion)
Splits responsibilities so dispatch, validation, sending, and audit are separated (single responsibility)
Refactored methods are short and at a single level of abstraction (composed method / SLAP)
Adding a new notification channel requires a new class, not editing NotificationService
